8. If we say we have no sin, we deceive ourselves, and the truth is not in us.
9. If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness.
10. If we say we have not sinned, we make him a liar, and his word is not in us.

1.notes:
1 John - Chapter 1
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
John declares the Word of life which was from the beginning — seen, heard, and touched — so that readers may have fellowship with the Father and Son, walking in the light where Christ's blood cleanses from all sin, and confessing sin to find faithful forgiveness.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (unnamed but identified by style, theology, and
early church tradition). The same author as the Gospel of John and Revelation.
Written approximately AD 85-95 from Ephesus, where John spent his later years.

Recipient: Believers in Asia Minor (likely churches around Ephesus). John
writes as an elderly pastor ("my little children") to a community threatened
by false teachers who denied Christ's true humanity (early Gnosticism).

Purpose: To give assurance of salvation (5:13), to combat false teaching about
Christ's nature, and to call believers to walk in the light, love one another,
and keep God's commandments. John provides "tests of life" — how to know you
truly know God.

Theme: Fellowship with God and assurance of salvation. Three tests repeated:
the doctrinal test (believing rightly about Christ), the moral test (obeying
God's commands), and the social test (loving one another).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-john-1/
 Guzik covers the eyewitness testimony, fellowship, God as light, walking
 in light, and the promise of confession and cleansing.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "If We Confess Our Sins" on v.9 (MTP Vol. 24, No. 1439)
 "If we CONFESS — not hide, not excuse, not minimize — confess. Agree with
 God about your sin. Call it what He calls it. He is FAITHFUL — He promised
 to forgive, and He keeps His promises. And JUST — the cross satisfied
 justice, so forgiveness is not unjust but righteous. To FORGIVE — pardon,
 release. And to CLEANSE — not just forgive but purify. From ALL
 unrighteousness — no sin too great, no stain too deep. Confess and be
 clean."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "handled" — Four sensory verbs: heard (akoō), seen (horaō), looked upon (theaomai — gazed at intently), handled/ touched (psēlaphaō — physically felt). John emphasizes: Christ was REAL, physical, tangible. Against Gnostics who denied His true humanity.
v.1               | ESV         | "touched" — Four sensory verbs: heard (akoō), seen (horaō), looked upon (theaomai — gazed at intently), handled/ touched (psēlaphaō — physically felt). John emphasizes: Christ was REAL, physical, tangible. Against Gnostics who denied His true humanity.
v.5               | KJV         | "God is light" — Not "God has light" or "God gives light" but God IS light. His essential nature is light — truth, purity, holiness. "No darkness at all" — Greek "ouk estin... oudemia" — absolutely none.
v.5               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Not "God has light" or "God gives light" but God IS light. His essential nature is light — truth, purity, holiness. "No darkness at all" — Greek "ouk estin... oudemia" — absolutely none.
v.7               | KJV         | "cleanseth" — Greek "katharizei" — present tense: keeps on cleansing. Ongoing, continuous purification as we walk in the light.
v.7               | ESV         | "cleanses" — Greek "katharizei" — present tense: keeps on cleansing. Ongoing, continuous purification as we walk in the light.
v.9               | KJV         | "faithful and just" — God's forgiveness is not arbitrary mercy but faithful promise-keeping and just response to Christ's atonement. Because Christ paid, God is JUST to forgive.
v.9               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — God's forgiveness is not arbitrary mercy but faithful promise-keeping and just response to Christ's atonement. Because Christ paid, God is JUST to forgive.
v.10              | KJV         | "we make him a liar" — Denying our sin contradicts God's word that all have sinned. It calls God a liar.
v.10              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Denying our sin contradicts God's word that all have sinned. It calls God a liar.
v.1               | NET         | "what existed from the beginning, what we have heard, what we have seen with our eyes" — four verbs of firsthand experience: heard, seen, observed, touched. WEB: "that which was from the beginning, that which we have heard." KJV: "That which was from the beginning." John stresses physical, sensory witness against Gnostic denial.
v.3               | NET         | "so that you may have fellowship with us (and indeed our fellowship is with the Father and with his Son)" — "fellowship" (koinOnia = shared participation/partnership). WEB: "our fellowship is with the Father, and with his Son." Horizontal fellowship is grounded in vertical fellowship.
v.5               | NET         | "God is light, and in him there is no darkness at all" — "no darkness at all" (skotia en autO ouk estin oudemia = double negative for emphasis). WEB: "in him is no darkness at all." Absolute moral purity — zero shadow.
v.7               | NET         | "if we walk in the light as he himself is in the light, we have fellowship with one another" — "walk in the light" (en tO phOti peripatOmen). WEB: "walk in the light." Walking = ongoing lifestyle. Light = transparency/holiness. Result = genuine community AND cleansing (v.7b).
v.9               | NET         | "he is faithful and righteous, forgiving us our sins and cleansing us from all unrighteousness" — "faithful and righteous" (pistos... dikaios) — forgiveness is not mercy contradicting justice but justice fulfilled through Christ. WEB: "faithful and righteous to forgive." God is JUST in forgiving because Christ paid.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Word of life      | Jesus Christ as the eternal Word who gives life. Both the                                                          | 
Fellowship        | Greek "koinōnia" — sharing, partnership, communion. Deep                                                           | 
God is light      | God's essential nature: truth, purity, holiness, goodness.                                                         | 
Walk in the light | Living in openness, honesty, and obedience. Not sinless                                                            | 
Confess           | Greek "homologeō" — to say the same thing, to agree with.                                                          | 
Faithful and just | God's character guaranteeing forgiveness. Faithful to                                                              | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "God is light, and in him is no darkness at all" (v.5). Is there
   darkness in your life that you're hiding from God? He is pure light —
   nothing is hidden from Him. Are you walking in the light or hiding in
   shadows?

2. "If we walk in the light... the blood of Jesus Christ his Son cleanseth
   us from all sin" (v.7). Walking in the light doesn't mean sinless
   perfection — it means living openly before God. When you do, Christ's
   blood keeps cleansing you. Are you walking openly?

3. "If we confess our sins" (v.9). Is there unconfessed sin in your life?
   Confession is agreeing with God — calling sin what He calls it. He
   promises to forgive AND cleanse. What do you need to confess today?

4. "If we say that we have no sin, we deceive ourselves" (v.8). Self-
   deception about sin is dangerous. Are you honest about your sinfulness?
   Or do you minimize, excuse, or deny?

5. "That your joy may be full" (v.4). John writes so that joy would be
   complete. Is your joy full? Fellowship with God and honest living
   produce full joy. What's blocking yours?

------------------------------------

David Guzik — 1 John 1 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-john-1/

  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

2.ESV:
1. My little children, I am writing these things to you so that you may not sin. But if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.
2. He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world.
3. And by this we know that we have come to know him, if we keep his commandments.
4. Whoever says “I know him” but does not keep his commandments is a liar, and the truth is not in him,
5. but whoever keeps his word, in him truly the love of God is perfected. By this we may know that we are in him:
6. whoever says he abides in him ought to walk in the same way in which he walked.
7. Beloved, I am writing you no new commandment, but an old commandment that you had from the beginning. The old commandment is the word that you have heard.
8. At the same time, it is a new commandment that I am writing to you, which is true in him and in you, because the darkness is passing away and the true light is already shining.
9. Whoever says he is in the light and hates his brother is still in darkness.
10. Whoever loves his brother abides in the light, and in him there is no cause for stumbling.
11. But whoever hates his brother is in the darkness and walks in the darkness, and does not know where he is going, because the darkness has blinded his eyes.
12. I am writing to you, little children, because your sins are forgiven for his name’s sake.
13. I am writing to you, fathers, because you know him who is from the beginning. I am writing to you, young men, because you have overcome the evil one. I write to you, children, because you know the Father.
14. I write to you, fathers, because you know him who is from the beginning. I write to you, young men, because you are strong, and the word of God abides in you, and you have overcome the evil one.
15. Do not love the world or the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him.
16. For all that is in the world—the desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride of life—is not from the Father but is from the world.
17. And the world is passing away along with its desires, but whoever does the will of God abides forever.
18. Children, it is the last hour, and as you have heard that antichrist is coming, so now many antichrists have come. Therefore we know that it is the last hour.
19. They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would have continued with us. But they went out, that it might become plain that they all are not of us.
20. But you have been anointed by the Holy One, and you all have knowledge.
21. I write to you, not because you do not know the truth, but because you know it, and because no lie is of the truth.
22. Who is the liar but he who denies that Jesus is the Christ? This is the antichrist, he who denies the Father and the Son.
23. No one who denies the Son has the Father. Whoever confesses the Son has the Father also.
24. Let what you heard from the beginning abide in you. If what you heard from the beginning abides in you, then you too will abide in the Son and in the Father.
25. And this is the promise that he made to us—eternal life.
26. I write these things to you about those who are trying to deceive you.
27. But the anointing that you received from him abides in you, and you have no need that anyone should teach you. But as his anointing teaches you about everything, and is true, and is no lie—just as it has taught you, abide in him.
28. And now, little children, abide in him, so that when he appears we may have confidence and not shrink from him in shame at his coming.
29. If you know that he is righteous, you may be sure that everyone who practices righteousness has been born of him.

5. But whoever keeps his word, God’s love has most certainly been perfected in him. This is how we know that we are in him:
6. he who says he remains in him ought himself also to walk just like he walked.
7. Brothers, I write no new commandment to you, but an old commandment which you had from the beginning. The old commandment is the word which you heard from the beginning.
8. Again, I write a new commandment to you, which is true in him and in you; because the darkness is passing away, and the true light already shines.
9. He who says he is in the light and hates his brother, is in the darkness even until now.
10. He who loves his brother remains in the light, and there is no occasion for stumbling in him.
11. But he who hates his brother is in the darkness, and walks in the darkness, and doesn’t know where he is going, because the darkness has blinded his eyes.
12. I write to you, little children, because your sins are forgiven you for his name’s sake.
13. I write to you, fathers, because you know him who is from the beginning. I write to you, young men, because you have overcome the evil one. I write to you, little children, because you know the Father.
14. I have written to you, fathers, because you know him who is from the beginning. I have written to you, young men, because you are strong, and the word of God remains in you, and you have overcome the evil one.
15. Don’t love the world or the things that are in the world. If anyone loves the world, the Father’s love isn’t in him.
16. For all that is in the world, the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life, isn’t the Father’s, but is the world’s.
17. The world is passing away with its lusts, but he who does God’s will remains forever.
18. Little children, these are the end times, and as you heard that the Antichrist is coming, even now many antichrists have arisen. By this we know that it is the final hour.
19. They went out from us, but they didn’t belong to us; for if they had belonged to us, they would have continued with us. But they left, that they might be revealed that none of them belong to us.
20. You have an anointing from the Holy One, and you all have knowledge.
21. I have not written to you because you don’t know the truth, but because you know it, and because no lie is of the truth.
22. Who is the liar but he who denies that Jesus is the Christ? This is the Antichrist, he who denies the Father and the Son.
23. Whoever denies the Son, the same doesn’t have the Father. He who confesses the Son has the Father also.
24. Therefore, as for you, let that remain in you which you heard from the beginning. If that which you heard from the beginning remains in you, you also will remain in the Son, and in the Father.
25. This is the promise which he promised us, the eternal life.
26. These things I have written to you concerning those who would lead you astray.
27. As for you, the anointing which you received from him remains in you, and you don’t need for anyone to teach you. But as his anointing teaches you concerning all things, and is true, and is no lie, and even as it taught you, you will remain in him.
28. Now, little children, remain in him, that when he appears, we may have boldness, and not be ashamed before him at his coming.
29. If you know that he is righteous, you know that everyone who practices righteousness has been born of him.
2.notes:
1 John - Chapter 2
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Jesus is our Advocate with the Father and the propitiation for sins, the one who truly knows God keeps His commandments, the new commandment is to love one another, and John warns against loving the world and the coming antichrists who deny the Father and the Son.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 2 addresses Christ as our advocate (vv.1-2), the
test of obedience (vv.3-6), the command to love (vv.7-11), assurance for
different maturity levels (vv.12-14), the warning against worldliness (vv.15-
17), and the threat of antichrists (vv.18-29). John continues providing "tests
of life" — how to know you truly know God.

Christ Our Advocate (vv.1-2): John writes so they won't sin — but when they
do, Christ is their advocate (defense attorney) with the Father (v.1). He is
the propitiation (satisfaction of God's wrath) for our sins and the whole
world's (v.2).

The Test of Obedience (vv.3-6): "We know that we know him, if we keep his
commandments" (v.3). Claiming to know God while disobeying is lying (v.4).
Walk as Christ walked (v.6).

The Command to Love (vv.7-11): The old/new commandment: love one another
(vv.7-8). Claiming to be in the light while hating a brother = still in
darkness (v.9). Loving = abiding in light (v.10). Hating = blinded by
darkness (v.11).

Warning Against Worldliness (vv.15-17): "Love not the world" (v.15). Three
categories of worldliness: lust of the flesh, lust of the eyes, pride of
life (v.16). The world passes away; doing God's will abides forever (v.17).

Antichrists (vv.18-29): Many antichrists have come (v.18). They left the
church — proving they were never truly part of it (v.19). The antichrist
denies that Jesus is the Christ (v.22). Believers have the "anointing" (the
Spirit) who teaches truth (vv.20, 27). Abide in Him (v.28).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-john-2/
 Guzik covers Christ as advocate, the obedience test, the love command,
 worldliness, and the antichrist warning.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "Love Not the World" on vv.15-17 (MTP Vol. 14, No. 834)
 "Love not the world — not the physical creation (God made that good) but
 the world-system opposed to God. Three forms: lust of the flesh (bodily
 appetites demanding satisfaction), lust of the eyes (covetous desire for
 what you see), pride of life (arrogant self-sufficiency). These three cover
 every temptation. Eve faced all three at the tree. Jesus faced all three in
 the wilderness. And the world passes away — it's temporary. Why love what's
 dying?"


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "advocate" — Greek "paraklēton" — one called alongside, a defense attorney. Same word Jesus used for the Holy Spirit (John 14:16). Christ pleads our case before the Father.
v.1               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "paraklēton" — one called alongside, a defense attorney. Same word Jesus used for the Holy Spirit (John 14:16). Christ pleads our case before the Father.
v.2               | KJV         | "propitiation" — Greek "hilasmos" — satisfaction, appeasement of wrath. Christ's sacrifice satisfied God's righteous anger against sin.
v.2               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "hilasmos" — satisfaction, appeasement of wrath. Christ's sacrifice satisfied God's righteous anger against sin.
v.15              | KJV         | "the world" — Greek "kosmos" — here meaning the world-system organized against God. Not the physical creation but the values, priorities, and pleasures opposed to God.
v.15              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "kosmos" — here meaning the world-system organized against God. Not the physical creation but the values, priorities, and pleasures opposed to God.
v.16              | KJV         | "lust of the flesh, lust of the eyes, pride of life" — Three categories: "lust/desires of the flesh" (epithumia tēs sarkos — bodily cravings), "lust/desires of the eyes" (epithumia tōn ophthalmōn — covetous desire), "pride of life" (alazoneia tou biou — arrogant boasting about possessions/status). Both translations equivalent.
v.16              | ESV         | "desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride of life" — Three categories: "lust/desires of the flesh" (epithumia tēs sarkos — bodily cravings), "lust/desires of the eyes" (epithumia tōn ophthalmōn — covetous desire), "pride of life" (alazoneia tou biou — arrogant boasting about possessions/status). Both translations equivalent.
v.18              | KJV         | "antichrist" — Greek "antichristos" — one who opposes or substitutes for Christ. Both a future figure and a present spirit manifested in "many antichrists."
v.18              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "antichristos" — one who opposes or substitutes for Christ. Both a future figure and a present spirit manifested in "many antichrists."
v.20              | KJV         | "unction from the Holy One" — Greek "chrisma" — anointing. The Holy Spirit given to believers, who teaches and confirms truth.
v.20              | ESV         | "anointed by the Holy One" — Greek "chrisma" — anointing. The Holy Spirit given to believers, who teaches and confirms truth.
v.28              | KJV         | "not be ashamed before him at his coming" — Greek "parousia" — coming, presence, arrival. The motivation for abiding: confidence at His return.
v.28              | ESV         | "not shrink from him in shame at his coming" — Greek "parousia" — coming, presence, arrival. The motivation for abiding: confidence at His return.
v.1               | NET         | "we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous one" — "advocate" (paraklEton = one called alongside/intercessor). WEB: "we have a Counselor with the Father." KJV: "we have an advocate with the Father." Same word Jesus used for the Spirit (John 14:16) — now applied to Himself.
v.2               | NET         | "he himself is the atoning sacrifice for our sins" — "atoning sacrifice" (hilasmos = propitiation/expiation). WEB: "the atoning sacrifice for our sins." KJV: "the propitiation for our sins." Does God's wrath need appeasing (propitiation) or sin need covering (expiation)? The word includes both.
v.15              | NET         | "Do not love the world or the things in the world" — "love" (agapate = don't set your affection on). WEB: "Don't love the world." KJV: "Love not the world." Not creation or people (John 3:16) but the world-SYSTEM opposed to God.
v.18              | NET         | "just as you heard that the antichrist is coming, so now many antichrists have appeared" — "many antichrists" (antichristoi polloi). WEB: "many antichrists have arisen." The spirit of antichrist manifests in many opponents before the final Antichrist — a present reality, not just future.
v.27              | NET         | "the anointing that you received from him resides in you, and you have no need for anyone to teach you" —  "anointing" (chrisma = unction). WEB: "the anointing which you received from him remains in you." The Holy Spirit's internal teaching — not rejecting human teachers but affirming the Spirit's authority.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Advocate          | Christ as our defense attorney before the Father. He pleads                                                        | 
Propitiation      | The satisfaction of God's wrath through Christ's sacrifice.                                                        | 
Love not the world| Refusing to adopt the world-system's values: bodily                                                                | 
Antichrist        | One who denies Christ. Both a future figure and a present                                                          | 
Anointing/Unction | The Holy Spirit's indwelling presence that teaches                                                                 | 
Abide in Him      | Remain in Christ. Continuous, ongoing relationship — not                                                           | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "We have an advocate with the Father" (v.1). When you sin, Christ
   doesn't condemn you — He defends you. Do you run to Him after failure or
   hide from Him?

2. "He that saith he abideth in him ought himself also so to walk, even as
   he walked" (v.6). Is your walk matching your talk? Does your life look
   like Jesus's life?

3. "Love not the world" (v.15). Which of the three worldly categories is
   your greatest temptation: lust of the flesh (bodily cravings), lust of
   the eyes (wanting what you see), or pride of life (status and self-
   sufficiency)?

4. "The world passeth away" (v.17). Everything the world offers is
   temporary. Are you investing in what lasts or what's passing away?

5. "They went out from us, but they were not of us" (v.19). Departure from
   the faith reveals that faith was never genuine. How does this both warn
   and comfort you?

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — 1 John Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/1-john/

David Guzik — 1 John 2 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-john-2/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

3.notes:
1 John - Chapter 3
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
"Behold, what manner of love the Father hath bestowed upon us, that we should be called the sons of God" — when He appears we shall be like Him, whoever is born of God does not practice sin, and we know we have passed from death to life because we love the brethren.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 3 explores what it means to be God's children
(vv.1-3), the incompatibility of sin with new birth (vv.4-10), the command
to love in action (vv.11-18), and assurance before God (vv.19-24). John draws
sharp contrasts: children of God vs. children of the devil, righteousness vs.
sin, love vs. hatred, life vs. death.

Children of God (vv.1-3): "Behold, what manner of love" — we are called
God's children (v.1). The world doesn't recognize us because it didn't
recognize Him (v.1). We don't yet know what we'll be — but when He appears,
"we shall be like him" (v.2). This hope purifies (v.3).

Sin and New Birth (vv.4-10): Sin is lawlessness (v.4). Christ came to take
away sins (v.5). Those who abide in Him don't practice sin (v.6). Those born
of God don't make sin their habit — God's seed remains in them (v.9). This
distinguishes God's children from the devil's (v.10).

Love in Action (vv.11-18): Love one another — not like Cain (vv.11-12). The
world hates us (v.13). Love proves we've passed from death to life (v.14).
Hatred = murder (v.15). Christ's love defined: He laid down His life (v.16).
We should do the same (v.16). If you have resources and see a brother in need
but don't help — how does God's love dwell in you? (v.17). "Let us not love
in word... but in deed and in truth" (v.18).

Assurance (vv.19-24): By loving in deed, we know we're of the truth (v.19).
If our heart condemns us, God is greater than our heart (v.20). If our heart
doesn't condemn us, we have confidence before God (v.21). His commandment:
believe in Christ and love one another (v.23).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "What Manner of Love" on v.1 (MTP Vol. 10, No. 581)
 "BEHOLD — stop and look. WHAT MANNER — what kind, what quality, what
 origin. Of LOVE — not duty, not obligation — love. The Father hath
 BESTOWED — given freely, lavished. That we should be CALLED — not just
 treated as but named, identified as. The SONS of God — not servants, not
 subjects — sons. This is not earned. This is not deserved. This is love

------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "what manner of love" — Greek "potapēn agapēn" — what kind of, what foreign/exotic love. A love so unusual it seems from another world. KJV: "sons of God" / ESV: "children of God" — Greek "tekna theou" — children (born of God).
v.1               | ESV         | "what kind of love" — Greek "potapēn agapēn" — what kind of, what foreign/exotic love. A love so unusual it seems from another world. KJV: "sons of God" / ESV: "children of God" — Greek "tekna theou" — children (born of God).
v.2               | KJV         | "we shall be like him" — Greek "homoioi autō esometha" — we will be similar to Him. Not identical (we don't become God) but transformed into His likeness.
v.2               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "homoioi autō esometha" — we will be similar to Him. Not identical (we don't become God) but transformed into His likeness.
v.6               | KJV         | "whosoever abideth in him sinneth not" — Greek "ouch hamartanei" — does not keep on sinning (present tense = habitual action). Not sinless perfection but sin is no longer the pattern/habit of life. ESV makes the continuous aspect clearer.
v.6               | ESV         | "no one who abides in him keeps on sinning" — Greek "ouch hamartanei" — does not keep on sinning (present tense = habitual action). Not sinless perfection but sin is no longer the pattern/habit of life. ESV makes the continuous aspect clearer.
v.9               | KJV         | "doth not commit sin" — Greek "hamartian ou poiei" — does not practice sin (present tense). "His seed" — God's nature/life implanted in the believer. "Cannot sin" — cannot make sin a lifestyle because God's nature within resists it.
v.9               | ESV         | "does not make a practice of sinning" — Greek "hamartian ou poiei" — does not practice sin (present tense). "His seed" — God's nature/life implanted in the believer. "Cannot sin" — cannot make sin a lifestyle because God's nature within resists it.
v.16              | KJV         | "hereby perceive we the love of God" — Greek "egnōkamen tēn agapēn" — we have come to know love. Love is defined by Christ's sacrifice — not by feelings but by self- giving action.
v.16              | ESV         | "by this we know love" — Greek "egnōkamen tēn agapēn" — we have come to know love. Love is defined by Christ's sacrifice — not by feelings but by self- giving action.
v.17              | KJV         | "shutteth up his bowels of compassion" — Greek "kleisē ta splagchna" — closes his inner feelings/compassion. "Bowels" in KJV = the seat of emotions in ancient thought.
v.17              | ESV         | "closes his heart against him" — Greek "kleisē ta splagchna" — closes his inner feelings/compassion. "Bowels" in KJV = the seat of emotions in ancient thought.
v.20              | KJV         | "God is greater than our heart" — When your conscience condemns you, God's grace is bigger than your guilt. He knows everything (more than your heart knows) and still loves you.
v.20              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — When your conscience condemns you, God's grace is bigger than your guilt. He knows everything (more than your heart knows) and still loves you.
v.1               | NET         | "See what sort of love the Father has given to us: that we should be called God's children" — "what sort" (potapEn = what country/what kind — amazement at its foreign quality). WEB: "See how great a love the Father has given to us." KJV: "Behold, what manner of love." This love is otherworldly — it doesn't originate on earth.
v.2               | NET         | "when he is revealed we will be like him, because we will see him just as he is" — "like him" (homoioi autO). WEB: "we will be like him; for we will see him just as he is." Seeing produces becoming — the beatific vision transforms the viewer.
v.9               | NET         | "Everyone who has been fathered by God does not practice sin" — "practice sin" (hamartian ou poiei = does not keep on sinning). WEB: "Whoever is born of God doesn't commit sin." KJV: "doth not commit sin." The present tense indicates habitual practice — not sinless perfection but a changed pattern of life.
v.14              | NET         | "We know that we have crossed over from death to life because we love our fellow Christians" — "crossed over" (metabebEkamen = have transferred/migrated). WEB: "We know that we have passed out of death into life." KJV: "We know that we have passed from death unto life." Perfect tense — a completed transfer with present results.
v.16              | NET         | "We have come to know love by this: that Jesus laid down his life for us" — "laid down" (ethEken = placed/set down deliberately). WEB: "he laid down his life for us." Love defined by action, not emotion — self-sacrificial giving is the measure.

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "What manner of love... that we should be called the sons of God" (v.1).
   Do you marvel at your adoption? Or has it become routine? Stop and
   behold the love that made you God's child.

2. "We shall be like him" (v.2). Your destiny is Christlikeness. Does this
   hope purify you now (v.3)? Does knowing where you're headed change how
   you live today?

3. "Whosoever is born of God doth not commit sin" (v.9). Is sin still your
   habit and pattern? Or has new birth produced a new direction? The test
   is not perfection but trajectory.

4. "Let us not love in word... but in deed and in truth" (v.18). Is your
   love practical or just verbal? Who needs your active, sacrificial love
   this week? What deed can you do?

5. "If our heart condemn us, God is greater than our heart" (v.20). When
   guilt overwhelms you, remember: God is bigger than your feelings. His
   grace is greater than your condemnation.

4.notes:
1 John - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
John commands believers to test the spirits (every spirit confessing Jesus Christ came in the flesh is from God), declares "God is love" and that His love was demonstrated in sending His Son as propitiation — "perfect love casteth out fear."


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 4 is the heart of 1 John — containing the
greatest statements about God's love in all of Scripture. It addresses testing
spirits/false prophets (vv.1-6) and then unfolds the nature of God's love and
its implications (vv.7-21). "God is love" (vv.8, 16) is the defining
declaration. The false teachers (Gnostics) claimed special spiritual knowledge
but lacked love; John shows that love is the true test of knowing God.

Testing the Spirits (vv.1-6): Don't believe every spirit — test them (v.1).
The test: does it confess Jesus Christ came in the flesh? (v.2). Denying
Christ's incarnation = the spirit of antichrist (v.3). "Greater is he that is
in you, than he that is in the world" (v.4). False teachers speak the world's
language and the world listens (v.5).

God Is Love (vv.7-21): Love one another — love is from God (v.7). "He that
loveth not knoweth not God; for God is love" (v.8). God's love demonstrated:
He sent His Son so we might live (v.9). Love defined: not that we loved God
but that He loved us and sent His Son as propitiation (v.10). If God so loved
us, we ought to love one another (v.11). God's love is perfected in us when
we love (v.12). "God is love; and he that dwelleth in love dwelleth in God"
(v.16). Perfect love casts out fear (v.18). "We love him, because he first
loved us" (v.19). You can't love God and hate your brother (v.20).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-john-4/
 Guzik covers testing spirits, God as love, love's definition, perfect love
 casting out fear, and the inseparability of loving God and loving brothers.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "God Is Love" on v.8 (MTP Vol. 7, No. 392)
 "God IS love — not God has love, not God shows love — God IS love. Love is
 not one of His attributes among many — it is His very essence. Everything
 He does flows from love. His justice is loving justice. His holiness is
 loving holiness. Even His wrath is the wrath of love against what destroys
 the beloved. And if God is love, then to know God is to know love, and to
 not love is to not know God."
 Sermon: "Perfect Love Casteth Out Fear" on v.18 (MTP Vol. 23, No. 1391)
 "Perfect love casteth OUT fear — not suppresses it, not manages it — casts
 it out. Evicts it. Fear and love cannot occupy the same heart. If you fear
 God's judgment, you haven't yet understood His love. If you fear His
 rejection, you haven't yet grasped His acceptance. Let His love fill you
 and fear will have no room to stay."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1               | KJV         | "try the spirits" — Greek "dokimazete ta pneumata" — test, examine, prove. The same word used for testing metals. Not gullibility but discernment.
v.1               | ESV         | "test the spirits" — Greek "dokimazete ta pneumata" — test, examine, prove. The same word used for testing metals. Not gullibility but discernment.
v.2               | KJV         | "Jesus Christ is come in the flesh" — Both equivalent. The incarnation test. The Gnostics denied Christ's true humanity. Confessing He came "in the flesh" = affirming genuine incarnation.
v.2               | ESV         | "Jesus Christ has come in the flesh" — Both equivalent. The incarnation test. The Gnostics denied Christ's true humanity. Confessing He came "in the flesh" = affirming genuine incarnation.
v.8               | KJV         | "God is love" — Greek "ho theos agapē estin." Not "God is loving" (an attribute) but "God IS love" (His essence). Love defines who God is at His core.
v.8               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "ho theos agapē estin." Not "God is loving" (an attribute) but "God IS love" (His essence). Love defines who God is at His core.
v.10              | KJV         | "propitiation" — Greek "hilasmon" — satisfaction, appeasement. God's love is not sentimental — it dealt with sin through sacrifice.
v.10              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Greek "hilasmon" — satisfaction, appeasement. God's love is not sentimental — it dealt with sin through sacrifice.
v.18              | KJV         | "perfect love casteth out fear" — Both equivalent. Greek "teleia agapē exō ballei ton phobon" — mature/complete love throws fear outside. KJV: "fear hath torment" / ESV: "fear has to do with punishment" — Greek "kolasin" — punishment. Fear anticipates punishment; love assures acceptance.
v.18              | ESV         | "perfect love casts out fear" — Both equivalent. Greek "teleia agapē exō ballei ton phobon" — mature/complete love throws fear outside. KJV: "fear hath torment" / ESV: "fear has to do with punishment" — Greek "kolasin" — punishment. Fear anticipates punishment; love assures acceptance.
v.19              | KJV         | "we love him, because he first loved us" — Greek "autos prōtos ēgapēsen hēmas" — He loved us first. God's love is the cause; ours is the response. He initiated; we respond. ESV omits "him" (some manuscripts lack it).
v.19              | ESV         | "we love because he first loved us" — Greek "autos prōtos ēgapēsen hēmas" — He loved us first. God's love is the cause; ours is the response. He initiated; we respond. ESV omits "him" (some manuscripts lack it).
v.1               | NET         | "do not believe every spirit, but test the spirits to determine if they are from God" — "test" (dokimazete = examine/prove/assay). WEB: "test the spirits." KJV: "try the spirits." Discernment is commanded, not optional — gullibility is not a fruit of the Spirit.
v.8               | NET         | "The one who does not love does not know God, because God is love" — "God is love" (ho theos agapE estin). WEB: "God is love." KJV: "God is love." Not "love is God" (making love ultimate) but "God is love" (love defined by God's character). God defines love, not the reverse.
v.10              | NET         | "In this is love: not that we have loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the atoning sacrifice for our sins" — love's definition: God's initiative, not our response. WEB: "sent his Son to be the atoning sacrifice." Love originates with God, not with us.
v.18              | NET         | "There is no fear in love, but perfect love drives out fear" — "drives out" (ballei = throws out/casts out — violent expulsion). WEB: "perfect love casts out fear." KJV: "perfect love casteth out fear." Love and fear cannot coexist — God's love evicts our terror of judgment.
v.19              | NET         | "We love because he first loved us" — "he first loved" (autos prOtos EgapEsen). WEB: "We love him, because he first loved us." Some manuscripts include "him" (KJV/WEB); others have just "we love" (NET/ESV). Either way: God's love precedes and enables ours.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Try/Test the spirits| Evaluate spiritual claims against the standard of                                                                  | 
God is love       | God's essential nature. Not merely an attribute but His                                                            | 
He first loved us | God's love as the initiator. Our love is always a                                                                  | 
Perfect love casteth out fear| Mature understanding of God's love eliminates                                                                      | 
Propitiation      | God's love expressed through satisfying His own justice.                                                           | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Try the spirits" (v.1). Do you test what you hear against Scripture?
   Not every spiritual-sounding message is from God. How do you discern
   truth from error?

2. "Greater is he that is in you, than he that is in the world" (v.4). The
   Spirit in you is greater than any force against you. Do you live in this
   confidence or in fear of the enemy?

3. "God is love" (v.8). Not just loving — love itself. How does this shape
   your understanding of everything God does — including discipline,
   silence, and allowing suffering?

4. "Perfect love casteth out fear" (v.18). Where does fear still grip you —
   fear of judgment, rejection, failure? Let God's love cast it out. You
   are loved; you are accepted; you are secure.

5. "We love him, because he first loved us" (v.19). Your love for God is a
   response to His love for you. Are you responding? Is His love producing
   love in you — for Him and for others?

5.notes:
1 John - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================


CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Whoever believes Jesus is the Christ is born of God, faith is the victory that overcomes the world, the Spirit and water and blood bear witness, and John writes "that ye may know that ye have eternal life" — closing with a warning against idols.


AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: The Apostle John (see Chapter 1 notes for full details).

Historical Context: Chapter 5 concludes 1 John with the themes of faith that
overcomes (vv.1-5), the witness to Christ (vv.6-12), assurance of eternal
life (v.13), confidence in prayer (vv.14-17), and three certainties (vv.18-
21). Verse 13 states the letter's purpose: "that ye may KNOW that ye have
eternal life." John wants believers to have assurance, not doubt.

Faith Overcomes the World (vv.1-5): Believing Jesus is the Christ = born of
God (v.1). Loving God + keeping commandments = loving God's children (v.2).
His commandments are not burdensome (v.3). "Whatsoever is born of God
overcometh the world" — the victory is our faith (v.4).

The Witness to Christ (vv.6-12): Christ came by water (baptism) and blood
(cross) — not water only (v.6). Three witnesses: Spirit, water, blood (v.8).
God's testimony about His Son is greater than human testimony (v.9). "He that
hath the Son hath life" (v.12).

Assurance and Prayer (vv.13-17): "That ye may KNOW that ye have eternal life"
(v.13). Confidence in prayer: ask according to His will and He hears (vv.14-
15). Praying for a brother who sins (vv.16-17).

Three Certainties (vv.18-21): (1) The born-again don't practice sin; the evil
one doesn't touch them (v.18). (2) We are of God; the world lies in the evil
one (v.19). (3) The Son of God has come and given us understanding (v.20).
Final word: "Keep yourselves from idols" (v.21).


COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
 https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/1-john-5/
 Guzik covers overcoming faith, the threefold witness, assurance, prayer,
 and the three certainties.

Charles Spurgeon:
 Sermon: "The Victory of Faith" on v.4 (MTP Vol. 11, No. 616)
 "This is the victory — not our works, not our feelings, not our efforts —
 our FAITH. Faith overcomes the world by looking beyond it to the unseen.
 Faith overcomes the world's temptations by seeing them as temporary. Faith
 overcomes the world's threats by trusting a sovereign God. The world is
 strong — but faith is stronger, because faith connects us to the One who
 has already overcome."
 Sermon: "Eternal Life" on vv.11-12 (MTP Vol. 34, No. 2048)
 "He that hath the Son HATH life. Present tense. Not 'will have' — hath.
 Right now. Eternal life is not just future — it is present possession. And
 it is IN the Son — not separate from Him. You don't get life apart from
 Christ. You get Christ, and life comes with Him. Have Him, have life. Don't
 have Him, don't have life. It's that simple."


TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.3               | KJV         | "his commandments are not grievous" — Greek "bareiai ouk eisin" — not heavy, not burdensome. God's commands are not oppressive — they are the path of freedom, not bondage.
v.3               | ESV         | "his commandments are not burdensome" — Greek "bareiai ouk eisin" — not heavy, not burdensome. God's commands are not oppressive — they are the path of freedom, not bondage.
v.4               | KJV         | "overcometh the world" — Greek "nikōsa" — conquers, is victorious over. Present tense: keeps on overcoming. "Our faith" — Greek "hē pistis hēmōn" — THE faith, our faith. Faith is the instrument of victory.
v.4               | ESV         | "overcomes the world" — Greek "nikōsa" — conquers, is victorious over. Present tense: keeps on overcoming. "Our faith" — Greek "hē pistis hēmōn" — THE faith, our faith. Faith is the instrument of victory.
v.6               | KJV         | "came by water and blood" — Christ's baptism (beginning of ministry) and cross (end of ministry). Against Gnostics who said the divine Christ left Jesus before the cross. No — He came by BOTH water AND blood.
v.6               | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — Christ's baptism (beginning of ministry) and cross (end of ministry). Against Gnostics who said the divine Christ left Jesus before the cross. No — He came by BOTH water AND blood.
v.7               | Multiple    | 8 — KJV includes the "Johannine Comma" (v.7b-8a): "the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost: and these three are one. And there are three that bear witness in earth" — ESV omits this, following most early Greek manuscripts. Most scholars consider it a later addition. The doctrine of the Trinity is established elsewhere regardless.
v.13              | KJV         | "that ye may KNOW that ye have eternal life" — Both equivalent. Greek "eidēte" — that you may know with certainty. John's stated purpose: assurance of salvation.
v.13              | ESV         | "that you may know that you have eternal life" — Both equivalent. Greek "eidēte" — that you may know with certainty. John's stated purpose: assurance of salvation.
v.16              | KJV         | "sin unto death" — Debated. Options: (1) physical death as discipline (cf. Acts 5, 1 Cor 11:30), (2) apostasy/blasphemy against the Spirit, (3) any sin that results in premature physical death.
v.16              | ESV         | "sin that leads to death" — Debated. Options: (1) physical death as discipline (cf. Acts 5, 1 Cor 11:30), (2) apostasy/blasphemy against the Spirit, (3) any sin that results in premature physical death.
v.19              | KJV         | "the whole world lieth in wickedness" — Greek "en tō ponērō keitai" — lies in the evil one. ESV makes the personal reference to Satan clearer.
v.19              | ESV         | "the whole world lies in the power of the evil one" — Greek "en tō ponērō keitai" — lies in the evil one. ESV makes the personal reference to Satan clearer.
v.21              | KJV         | "keep yourselves from idols" — An abrupt ending. Anything that takes God's place is an idol. After a letter about knowing the true God, the final word: don't substitute anything for Him.
v.21              | ESV         | "Both equivalent." — An abrupt ending. Anything that takes God's place is an idol. After a letter about knowing the true God, the final word: don't substitute anything for Him.
v.3               | NET         | "For this is the love of God: that we keep his commandments. And his commandments do not weigh us down" — "do not weigh us down" (bareiai ouk eisin = are not heavy/ burdensome). WEB: "his commandments are not grievous." KJV: "his commandments are not grievous." Obedience is not oppressive for the born-again — love makes duty light.
v.4               | NET         | "this is the conquering power that has conquered the world: our faith" — "conquering power" (nikE = victory). WEB: "this is the victory that has overcome the world: our faith." KJV: "this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith." Faith IS the victory — not produces it but IS it.
v.7               | NET         | "For there are three that testify" — the longer reading (Comma Johanneum: "in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost") is found in KJV but NOT in NET/ESV/WEB/ ASV. It is absent from all Greek manuscripts before the 16th century. NET omits it following all early manuscript evidence.
v.13              | NET         | "I have written these things to you who believe in the name of the Son of God so that you may know that you have eternal life" — "that you may KNOW" (hina eidEte = that you may have settled knowledge). WEB: "that you may know that you have eternal life." Assurance is possible and intended — John writes for certainty, not doubt.
v.21              | NET         | "Little children, guard yourselves from idols" — "guard yourselves" (phylaxate heauta = keep/protect yourselves). WEB: "Little children, keep yourselves from idols." KJV: "Little children, keep yourselves from idols." The abrupt ending — the opposite of loving God is idolatry. A final warning.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                                                                  | Notes
------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Overcometh the world| Faith's victory over the world's temptations, values,                                                              | 
He that hath the Son hath life| Eternal life is inseparable from Christ.                                                                           | 
That ye may know  | John's purpose: certainty of salvation. Not arrogant                                                               | 
According to His will| The condition for confident prayer. Aligning our                                                                   | 
Sin unto death    | A sin that results in physical death as divine discipline.                                                         | 
Keep from idols   | Anything that replaces God in your affection, trust, or                                                            | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "This is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith" (v.4).
   Is your faith overcoming the world — its temptations, its values, its
   pressure? Or is the world overcoming you?

2. "He that hath the Son hath life" (v.12). Do you have the Son? Then you
   have life — right now, present tense. Are you living in that assurance?

3. "That ye may KNOW that ye have eternal life" (v.13). Do you know? Not
   hope, not wish — know. John wrote so you could have certainty. What
   gives you assurance?

4. "If we ask any thing according to his will, he heareth us" (v.14). Are
   your prayers aligned with God's will? How do you discern His will so you
   can pray with confidence?

5. "Keep yourselves from idols" (v.21). What idols compete for God's place
   in your life — money, comfort, approval, control, entertainment? The
   final word of 1 John: guard against substitutes.
